segments.s656-g225: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' placed before the person asked; دانای هندی is a noun phrase 'the Indian sage' (دانا used as noun 'sage'). زمان is the thing requested and appears before the verb (typical Persian SOV flexibility). خواستیم is past tense, 1st-person plural of خواستن, here meaning 'we asked/requested'.
segments.s656-g226: به دانش introduces the instrument/means or the content 'with/through knowledge' (به + noun). روان را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker marking 'the soul' as the object. بیاراستیم is a past tense verb (1st-person plural) built on آراستن 'to adorn/arrange' and here means 'we adorned/arrayed'; it takes روان را as its object.
segments.s656-g227: بسی (classical adverb) intensifies رای 'opinion/counsel' ('many' or 'much counsel'). رای زد is a light-verb construction: رای (noun) + زد (past of زدن) meaning 'uttered/made a counsel/decision'. موبد پاک‌رای is a noun phrase 'the mōbed, pure-counseled' (= virtuous or of pure reputation) placed after the predicate (poetic word order).
segments.s656-g228: پژوهید is past tense (3rd-person singular) of پژوهیدن 'to examine/investigate' ('he examined/pondered'). وآورد is the conjunction + past-verb 'and brought' (و + آورد) connecting the next action. بازی به جای is a prepositional phrase where بازی 'the game' is placed before به جای 'into/into its place', so و آورد بازی به جای = 'and he brought/set the game in place.'
segments.s656-g229: کنون is an adverb 'now/presently' placed before the verb. آمد is the past tense 3rd-person singular 'came'. این موبد هوشمند is the noun phrase 'this mōbed, intelligent' serving as the subject; هوشمند is an adjective after the noun (typical Persian order).
segments.s656-g230: به قنوج نزدیک is a directional/adverbial phrase 'near to Kannauj' where به marks direction and نزدیک qualifies proximity. رای بلند is a noun + adjective sequence meaning 'lofty counsel' (رای = counsel/plan; بلند = lofty/high) describing the manner or quality accompanying his coming.
segments.s656-g231: شتروار is an adjectival/adverbial formation from شتر 'camel' meaning 'in camel-train fashion' or 'caravan-wise' and qualifies بار 'load/cargo'. بار گران is a noun phrase 'heavy load' (بار + adjective گران). The numerals 'دو هزار' follow in apposition to quantify the loads (classical poetic placement).
segments.s656-g232: پسندیده بار is 'chosen/pleasing cargo' (پسندیده qualifying بار). از در introduces source 'from the door/gate of' where در here means 'threshold/gate' (a poetic idiom meaning the court/house of). شهریار is the possessor 'the king' (genitive relation implied: 'the king's gate').
segments.s656-g81: This clause strings three past-tense verbs together with subjects implicit: یکی (an indefinite 'one/someone') is the subject, گفت (past 'said') and وپرسید ('and asked' — the conjunction v- is prefixed) are coordinated actions, and دیگر ('another') is the following subject for شنید ('heard'). The verbs are simple past (3rd person) and the short clause is a compressed, poetic narrative sequence.
segments.s656-g82: نیاورد is a past transitive verb 'did not bring/produce' placed before its subject (a common poetic order with negation). کس here is a negative/indefinite subject 'no one'. راه بازی forms a noun phrase 'a way/method of the game', and پدید (literally 'manifest/appear') functions here as part of the verbal idea tied to نیاورد — i.e. 'no one produced/found a way for the game (to be done)'.
segments.s656-g83: برفتند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they went/left'. یکسر is an adverb meaning 'all at once' or 'completely' modifying the action, and پرآژنگ is a compound adjective 'ornamented/splendid' modifying چهر ('face/appearance'). The sequence is compact and poetic: verb + adverb + adjective + noun describe the departing group.
segments.s656-g84: بیامد is the simple past 'came'. برشاه is a prepositional phrase 'to/upon the king' (here meaning 'in the presence of the king' or 'before the king'), and بوزرجمهر is the personal name (subject in inverted poetic order). The order (verb + prepositional phrase + name) is a common poetic inversion meaning 'Bozorgmehr came to the king.'
segments.s656-g85: ورا is a prepositional/pronominal phrase meaning 'to him' or 'for him' (dative). زان 'from that' points back to the previously mentioned words/speech, and سخن نیک 'good/fair speech' is the noun phrase. ناکام is a predicate adjective 'unsuccessful/failed', and دید is the past 'saw/perceived'. The clause means 'he perceived for himself that that good speech was unsuccessful.'
segments.s656-g86: به آغاز (literally 'at the beginning') is a temporal phrase; آن رنج 'that suffering/hardship' specifies which beginning, and فرجام دید 'he saw the outcome' places دید as the main past verb. Word order is temporal phrase → noun → object → verb: 'at the outset of that hardship he saw the outcome.'
segments.s656-g87: به کسری 'to Kisra' (Khosrow) marks the indirect object — 'he said to Khosrow'. چنین گفت is the common formula 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so'). کای is the vocative particle 'O', and پادشا is 'king' — together they introduce a direct address. گفت here functions as the reporting verb (past), and the vocative follows it.
segments.s656-g88: This is a string of vocative epithets addressing the king: جهاندار ('sovereign/conqueror'), و (and), بیدار ('awake/alert/vigilant'), و (and), فرمانروا ('ruler'). In context they are coordinated nouns/adjectives forming the formal address that follows کای پادشا.
segments.s656-g57: همان (demonstrative) modifies باژ: 'that very / the same cry'. باید + پذیرفت is an impersonal/modal construction: باید ('one must') + the infinitive/present-stem پذیرفت ('accept') yields 'must accept'. نیز ('also/likewise') links this clause to what follows, marking that this acceptance is to be added to other points just made.
segments.s656-g58: که introduces a reason/explanatory clause here ('for / because'). دانش is the subject/nominative of the clause ('knowledge'). به ... از is a comparative structure ('behtar ... az' in modern Persian), here compacted: به ... از = 'better than'. نامبردار ('renowned, celebrated') qualifies چیز ('thing'); together: 'knowledge is better than the celebrated thing'.
segments.s656-g59: دل و گوش کسری is a coordinated object ('the heart and ear of Kisra' = his attention and listening). بگوینده is the indirect object ('to the speaker') and داد is the past-tense verb 'gave.' Word order is object + indirect object + verb (typical of Persian poetic syntax): 'he gave Kisra's heart and ear to the speaker' i.e. the king listened attentively.
segments.s656-g60: سخنها ('the words/tales') is the theme; برو کرد here is a light/periphrastic verbal sequence where برو (an older/formal particle meaning roughly 'onwards' / 'began to') combines with کرد ('did/made') to mean 'proceeded to' or 'began to do' — so 'the speaker proceeded to speak the words'. گوینده ('the speaker') names the agent, and یاد ('from memory/recall') indicates that the recitation comes from remembrance.
segments.s656-g61: نهادند is a past plural verb 'they placed/set'. شطرنج is the object 'a chessboard'. نزدیک شاه is a locative adjunct 'near the king'. The sequence is verb-final poetic order with the locative following the noun: 'They placed the chessboard near the king.'
segments.s656-g62: به مهره ('at the pieces') + درون کرد چندی نگاه: درون کرد here functions causatively/putting sense with regard to the pieces on the board (i.e. the pieces that were set on/within it), and چندی نگاه ('a short while's gaze') says he looked for a while. Overall: 'He looked for a while at the pieces that had been put on it.' Note the compact poetic ordering: object phrase (به مهره درون) before the verb (کرد) and temporal adjunct (چندی نگاه).
segments.s656-g63: ز تختش ('from/on his throne') gives the provenance or location. یکی مهره ('one piece') is the subject and از عاج بود is the predicate: 'was of ivory.' بود is past copula 'was.' The clause describes one of the pieces as made from ivory.
segments.s656-g64: پر از رنگ ('full of color' = richly colored) modifies پیکر دگر ('another figure'). ساج بود ('was of sāj') gives its material: sāj here is the name of a kind of wood. The phrase thus contrasts two pieces: one ivory, the other a colored wooden figure.
segments.s656-g241: This clause begins with an impersonal necessity construction 'باید که' ('it is necessary that / they must') where the real verb appears later; 'شتروار' is adverbial/qualifying ('in camel-train / in file') and precedes the copular/necessity phrase. 'هم' (= also/even) emphasizes inclusion. 'زین شمار' is a noun phrase: 'زین' here is the noun 'saddle' and 'شمار' is 'number/count', together meaning 'this number of saddles' or 'of this many saddles' (classical/poetic order places the modifier before the head).
segments.s656-g242: This clause completes the necessity begun earlier: after 'باید که' an action is expected, expressed by the verb 'کند' (here 'to make / to do'). 'به پیمان کند' = 'to make a pact/pledge'; 'رای' here means 'plan / counsel / decision' and governs what is decided; 'قنوج' is a place name (Kannauj) and 'بار' is 'load/cargo' — the phrase means to decide on a load destined for Kannauj. The verb 'کند' is the action required by the preceding 'باید که'.
segments.s656-g243: 'کند' is the verb 'makes/does' (here 'takes/sets' a load). The first 'بار' is the direct object ('a load/cargo'). 'همراه' followed by 'با' indicates accompaniment ('together/along with'); the second 'بار ما' is 'our load'. The sequence shows two coordinated objects: he loads (or carries) a load together with our load.
segments.s656-g244: 'چنینست' is a present copular/affirmative formula meaning 'it is thus / such is'. It introduces a summary or declaration about what follows: 'پیمان و بازار ما' ('our covenant and our market') — a verbless nominal clause typical of Persian poetry where the copula is explicit in 'چنینست'. The coordination 'پیمان و بازار' links two nouns as objects of the comment.
segments.s656-g245: 'چو' here is temporal ('when'); 'خورشید' is the subject 'the sun'. 'رخشنده شد' is a compound: 'رخشنده' (radiant) + past-of-become 'شد' = 'became radiant / shone'. 'بر سپهر' is a prepositional phrase 'over/on the heaven/sky' where 'بر' marks location over the sky. The whole clause is a temporal subordinate clause setting the scene.
segments.s656-g246: This clause gives the main action: the intended verb is 'رفت' (went forth), here written as the poetic form 'برفت' (went up/out). The provided lemma/senses for this token are mismatched (they list 'snow'), so the correct verbal sense is not available in the provided list — accordingly that word's sense is marked -1. 'از در' = 'from the gate/door' ('در' = gate/door here), 'شاه' = 'the king', and 'بوزرجمهر' is the proper name Bozorgmehr; together 'برفت از در شاه بوزرجمهر' = 'he went forth from the king Bozorgmehr's gate' (i.e., Bozorgmehr left the king's gate).
segments.s656-g247: 'چو آمد' = 'when he came' (temporal 'when' + past verb). 'ز ایران' = 'from Iran' (poetic 'ز' = 'from'). 'به نزدیک رای' — 'به' plus 'نزدیک' (near/approaching) plus 'رای' (here 'counsel/decision') form an expression meaning 'to approach for counsel' or 'to come near for deliberation'; syntactically 'رای' links on to the next segment where the adviser (برهمن) is named. The clause thus sets the purpose/place of coming: to seek counsel.
segments.s656-g248: 'برهمن' is the person approached (a Brahmin/adviser). 'بشادی' is an adverbial phrase 'with joy / joyfully'. 'ورا' = 'to him / toward him' (archaic/thr direction), and 'رهنمای' is a noun meaning 'guide / adviser' — the clause says he joyfully approached the Brahmin and made him (or accepted him as) his guide/adviser. Word order is typical of Persian: object/goal follows the verb of motion and purpose.
segments.s656-g281: مگر introduces a restrictive condition here meaning 'unless'/'only if'. با + روان + یار گردد is a compact predicate: با (with) + روان (the soul) + یار گردد (گردد = 'become') — literally 'becomes a companion with the soul'. خرد, the noun 'wisdom', appears at the end as the semantic subject that would become the companion.
segments.s656-g282: کزین begins a relative/explanatory clause 'from this' (کز + این). مهره بازی is a compound meaning 'bead-playing' or 'trivial game' (مهره = bead/game-piece + بازی = play). برون آورد is a transitive perfective phrase 'brings out / takes out' (آورد = 'brought/bring'). The clause qualifies the condition in the first segment: wisdom must be brought out of this play.
segments.s656-g283: بیامد is a simple past 'came/arrived'. نهم روز ('ninth day') precedes the personal name in classical word order: 'the ninth day came Bozorgmehr' — i.e. 'on the ninth day Bozorgmehr arrived.' بوزرجمهر is the proper name of the character.
segments.s656-g284: پر از + آرزو + دل = 'a heart full of desire/longing' (پر از = 'full of', دل = 'heart'). The following compound پرآژنگ چهر places the adjective-like element پرآژنگ ('ornamented/resplendent') before چهر ('face') — a poetic order meaning 'his face richly/ornamentally adorned.' These two noun phrases describe Bozorgmehr's appearance and inner state.
segments.s656-g285: که here introduces the explanatory/reason clause ('for/because'). کسری is the king (Kisra/Khosrow). نفرمود is the past negative of فرمود ('he did not say/command'); followed by ما را (object 'us' with the object marker را) and درنگ ('delay/tarry'). The clause means 'for Khosrow did not tell/command us to delay.'
segments.s656-g286: نباید is an impersonal modal 'it must not / one should not'. The pattern نباید که + verb expresses 'it should not be that ...' or 'we must not allow ...'. گردد is the present/subjunctive form of 'become'. دل شاه تنگ ('the king's heart narrow') uses تنگ idiomatically = 'sorrowful/anxious.' Together: 'we must not let the king's heart become anxious.'
segments.s656-g287: بشد is a past/ narrative form 'it became/it happened' (here 'they became'). موبدان را uses را to mark the affected persons ('the mobeds' / priests). ازان دل دژم = 'from that (event) [their] heart(s) sullen' — ازان ('from that') indicates cause, دل دژم ('sullen heart') describes the resulting state. Word order is inverted in poetical narrative: verb + affected person + cause phrase.
segments.s656-g288: This line gives two parallel descriptive noun phrases: روان پر زغم = 'the spirit/soul full of sorrow' (روان + پر = 'full of' + زغم = 'sorrow'), and ابروان پر زخم = 'the eyebrows/brows full of wounds' (a figurative description of suffering or furrowed, scarred brows). The repeated construction 'X پر Y' means 'X full of Y' in poetic usage.
segments.s656-g361: شگفتی‌تر is the comparative adjective 'more astonishing/stranger' (تر = comparative suffix). از introduces the standard of comparison ('than'). کار بوزرجمهر is a genitival noun phrase 'the deed/work of Bozorgmehr' (name after the noun). Together this forms the comparative phrase 'more astonishing than the deed of Bozorgmehr,' which the following clause (introduced by که) will complete.
segments.s656-g362: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('that'). دانش is the direct object 'knowledge,' بدو is the dative clitic 'to him,' and داد is the simple past 'gave.' Poetic word order places چندین سپهر ('several/many heavens') after the verb as the subject; so the clause means 'that many heavens gave him knowledge.'
segments.s656-g363: سپاس is a noun meaning 'thanks/praise.' از here marks the beneficiary/source ('to/for' or 'from'), and خداوند خورشید وماه is a title phrase 'the Lord (of) sun and moon' (noun followed by its attributes/objects). The whole segment expresses 'praise/thanks to the Lord of sun and moon.'
segments.s656-g364: کزویست is an archaic fused form (کز + وی + است = 'that/from him is') functioning like 'from whom/which is.' It links to the following nouns: پیروزی و دستگاه are the things that 'are from him' — 'victory and دستگاه (dominion/court).' In modern Persian this would be rendered 'که از وی است' or 'که از اوست.'
segments.s656-g365: برین is the contracted prepositional phrase بر + این = 'on/at this' (here 'this' story). داستان is 'tale/story.' برسخن ('بر سخن') means 'into speech/into words' or 'in spoken/formal speech.' ساختم is 1st person singular past of ساختن 'I made/composed.' Together: 'I composed/expressed this tale in words/speech.'
segments.s656-g366: به is a preposition 'with/by/using.' طلخند ('تلخ + خند' in poetic spelling) is a wry or bitter smile/laugh, و is 'and,' شطرنج is 'chess.' پرداختم is 1st person singular past of پرداختن ('I attended to/treated/engaged in'), so the phrase means 'I handled/treated (it) with wry smiles and chess' — i.e., I adorned or presented it using wry laughter and the game of chess.
segments.s656-g137: برتخت is a prepositional/locative compound meaning 'to/at the throne' and comes first to mark the destination/place; آن شاه بیداربخت follows in the genitive/identifying sequence ('that/ the ill‑fated king'). The phrase as a whole functions as the indirect object/location of the verb that follows (i.e. 'brought to the throne of that ill‑fated king').
segments.s656-g138: بیاورد is a past stem meaning 'he brought' (simple past narrative); و is the coordinating 'and'; بنهاد is another past/stative verb 'he placed/put down' and links by و to the previous action. شطرنج و تخت are the objects of بنهاد — 'chess' (the game/board) and 'the throne' (or 'board/throne' depending on nuance) — presented after the verb in normal Persian object position.
segments.s656-g139: چنین here adverbially means 'thus' or 'so' and sets the manner of speaking; گفت is the speech verb (here used in narrative 'he said' / 'he spoke'); با introduces the people addressed ('with the mōbedān and the rādān') and lists them in the normal associative order. Grammatically this segment frames the speech act and names the addressees.
segments.s656-g140: که‌ای is a vocative/introducing particle here ('O' or 'that/you who are'), used to call attention to those being spoken to; نامور پاک‌دل بخردان is a string of epithets in the vocative plural 'renowned, pure‑hearted, wise ones' (adjectives precede the noun/epithet, a common poetic order). The segment functions as the opening address of the quoted speech.
segments.s656-g141: همه گوش دارید is an idiomatic present imperative/command construction: همه 'all' + گوش 'ear/attention' + دارید (2nd‑person plural present of داشتن) meaning 'have/hold' — i.e. 'all of you, pay attention' or 'lend your ears'. گفتار اوی places the possessed noun after it (گفتار 'speech/words' + اوی 'his'), so 'listen to his words.'
segments.s656-g142: هم at the start means 'also/indeed' and connects this clause to the previous command; آن را is a demonstrative/topic sequence ('that one' with را as the object/emphasis marker). هشیار سالار اوی is a noun phrase modifying that demonstrative: هشیار 'alert' + سالار 'commander/chief' + اوی 'his', i.e. 'that his vigilant commander.' The segment issues an injunction concerning that particular commander (be alert/attend).
segments.s656-g143: بیاراست is a past/preverbal stem meaning 'adorned/arranged/prepared' (here used as a transitive action); دانا 'the wise one' is the agent, followed by یکی رزمگاه 'a certain/some battlefield' where یکی functions as the indefinite 'a certain' before the noun. Word order is verb‑agent‑object in this poetic line, with the verb fronted for style.
segments.s656-g144: به introduces beneficiary/location 'for/to' (به قلب اندرون = 'in/to the heart of the interior'); قلب اندرون is a genitive/compound 'the heart (core) of the inner quarters' and ساخته is a perfect/participial form 'made/constructed' modifying جای شاه 'the place of the king' (جای 'place' + شاه 'king'). The clause thus states that he constructed a place for the king in the inner quarters.
segments.s656-g297: ازو (az(u)) is a prepositional/causal phrase 'because of him / on account of him' placed before the verb for emphasis. خیره شد is a compound intransitive past: خیره (astonished) + شد (become) = 'became astonished.' رای با رای‌زن is the grammatical subject (or the thing affected): رای (counsel/opinion) coordinated with رای‌زن (the counselor) by با ('with'). The verb شد is 3rd-person past singular here used generically.
segments.s656-g298: ز introduces a genitive/qualifying phrase 'of/from' in a poetic register. کشور بسی نامدار انجمن is a postpositive description modifying the preceding people/things: کشور ('realm') + بسی (an intensifier) + نامدار ('renowned') + انجمن ('assembly'). Word order is noun → modifiers; the whole phrase qualifies which assembly/counselors are meant.
segments.s656-g299: همه مهتران is the subject: 'all the chiefs/elders' (مهتران = leaders/important men in older usage). آفرین خواندند is a past plural action: آفرین ('praise' or exclamation) + خواندند ('they uttered/sang/declared'); here خواندند denotes they exclaimed or loudly expressed the acclamation.
segments.s656-g300: ورا is the obiect pronoun 'to him / at him' (they directed the action toward him). موبد پاک دین خواندند is a nominal phrase with خواندند used as 'they called/named (him)' — they gave him the title موبد پاک دین ('the mōbed pure of religion'), where پاک qualifies موبد and دین specifies the sort of purity (religious).
segments.s656-g301: ز هر دانشی is a prepositional phrase 'from every knowledge/branch of learning' (literary order). زو بپرسید رای literally 'they asked counsel of him': زو = 'from him/of him' (object of asking), بپرسید is the past 3rd-person form used generically ('asked'), and رای sits as the thing requested ('counsel/opinion'). Note the verb follows its object phrases in this poetic word order.
segments.s656-g302: همه پاسخ آمد is a statement with a collective subject and a singular past verb: 'all the answers came.' یکایک ('one by one' / 'each and every') plus به جای ('in place' / 'in their proper place') form an adverbial phrase describing how the answers arrived — orderly and individually.
segments.s656-g303: خروشی برآمد is a standard collocation: خروشی ('a shout/roar/commotion') + برآمد ('arose/issued forth') = 'a shout arose.' ز دانندگان marks the source: 'from the knowledgeable/experts.' The verb is past (simple perfective) and the preposition ز gives the origin.
segments.s656-g304: ز introduces a coordinated list of groups who raised the shout: دانش پژوهان ('seekers/researchers of learning') و خوانندگان ('and the readers/reciters'). The conjunction is standard; the whole phrase specifies the classes of people making the noise.
segments.s656-g265: ز گفتار is a causal/prepositional phrase ('because of / from the talk'); ز here gives the cause. اوشد is an archaic/poetic past-form meaning 'became' (third-person singular). رخ شاه زرد is a normal noun phrase 'the king's face (became) yellow/pale' with the adjective زرد after the noun; the whole segment states the resulting change of the king's appearance.
segments.s656-g266: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is past tense 'he heard' with the subject omitted (contextually the king). گفتار شطرنج و نرد is the object phrase 'talk/speech about chess and backgammon' (شطرنج و نرد linked by و). The clause gives the time/circumstance causing the change in the previous clause.
segments.s656-g267: بیامد is the past tense 'came' placed at the clause start (poetic word order). یکی نامور is an indefinite + adjective ('a certain renowned ...'), with نامور postpositive as is common in poetry. کدخدای is the noun 'kadhkhoda / village headman' as the main referent.
segments.s656-g268: فرستاده را is the definite object marked by را ('the envoy'). داد is the past 'gave'. شایسته‌جای is the direct complement 'a fitting/place proper for him'. The structure yields 'he gave the envoy a fitting place' (object + را before the verb, complement after the verb).
segments.s656-g269: یکی خرم ایوان is an object phrase 'a certain/one joyful/pleasant iwan' (یکی indefinite + adjective خرم). بیاراستند is the past plural 'they adorned/they set in order' — verb agrees with the plural subject implied by context (they/the attendants).
segments.s656-g270: This is a list of what they called for: می‌و and رود (joined by و) followed by رامشگران and the verb خواستند 'they desired/they summoned'. خواستند is past plural 'they asked for/called for'. Note that some lexical items are archaic/ambiguous here (see senses): the clause means they brought or ordered wine, musical instruments, and entertainers.
segments.s656-g271: زمان خواست is 'he/they asked for time' with زمان (time) as the object before the verb خواست (past 'asked/wanted'). پس is an adverb 'then/after that'. نامور هفت روز functions as a temporal specification indicating the length requested ('the renowned [one] asked for seven days' time).
segments.s656-g272: برفت is an older past form meaning 'went away/left'. آنک is a relative/demonstrative 'those who/which'; بودند is past plural 'were'. دانش فروز is a compound adjective/noun phrase 'knowledge‑shining' meaning 'bright in learning' or 'learned'. Together: 'those who were bright in learning left.'
segments.s656-g177: یکی here functions as an indefinite numeral ('a certain' / 'one') introducing the object; رزمگه (compound: رزم + گه) is the direct object 'battlefield/arena' and precedes the verb in the flexible Persian word order; ساخت is a simple past tense verb 'made/built'; شطرنج وار is a postpositive modifier: شطرنج 'chess/chessboard' + وار suffix meaning '-like', creating 'chessboard-like'.
segments.s656-g178: دو رویه is a compound meaning 'two-sided' or 'two faces/lines' (رویه here = side/face); برآراسته is a perfect/past participle used verbally as 'set up/arranged' (بر‑ prefix + آراسته); کارزار ('battle/engagement') is the noun being modified, so the phrase reads 'two-sided arranged battle' → 'a battle arranged in two opposing ranks'.
segments.s656-g179: دولشکر ('two-hosts') is the noun phrase acting as the object/thing being apportioned; ببخشید is the past action 'allotted/divided' (an older/poetic form of بخشیدن used as a transitive past); بر هشت بهر is a prepositional phrase 'into eight portions' where بر + numeral + بهر expresses division into parts.
segments.s656-g180: همه 'all' modifies رزمجویان 'the warriors' (plural); گیرنده here functions as an agentive/participial label 'the ones who take/are to take' (i.e., the captors/assailants), modifying شهر 'city' in the sense 'warriors assigned to take the city'—the noun phrase is understood as 'all the warriors (who will) seize each city'.
segments.s656-g181: زمین وار is a simile: زمین 'ground/earth' + وار suffix '-like', so 'spread like the earth' i.e. covering the ground; لشکر 'the host/army' is the subject; گهی 'sometimes' is an adverb modifying the verb implied earlier (the verb of spreading), and چارسوی 'in the four directions' (چارسوی = four‑ways) gives the extent—together: 'the army, like the earth, at times in four directions'.
segments.s656-g182: دوشاه 'two kings' is a nominal phrase; گرانمایه is an attributive adjective 'esteemed/precious' and نیک خوی is a compound adjective phrase 'good‑disposed / of good character' (نیک 'good' + خوی 'disposition'), joined by و 'and'—standard noun + adjectival modifiers order.
segments.s656-g183: کم و بیش is an idiomatic adverbial phrase 'more or less'; دارند is present-tense 'they have/possess' (here functioning stative: 'they are characterized by'); هر دو 'both' is the subject and به هم 'toward one another / with each other' indicates reciprocity—so the clause reads 'both, more or less, have (the same) toward one another.'
segments.s656-g184: یکی 'one' (here 'either/one of them') + از دگر 'against/from the other' sets up the negative action; برنگیرد is a negative present/subjunctive/formal older form meaning 'would not turn/turn toward' (here 'would not resort to' or 'would not inflict'), and ستم 'oppression/injustice' is the object—thus 'one would not bring injustice upon the other' (i.e., neither would wrong the other).
segments.s656-g17: برسم بزرگان is an idiomatic, slightly archaic phrase equivalent to modern به رسم بزرگان, meaning 'according to the custom/tradition of the great ones'; برسم here behaves like a prepositional/adi- locution. نیایش گرفت is a light-verb construction: نیایش (noun 'prayer') + گرفت (past tense of گرفتن used idiomatically as 'to perform/offer' a ritual action). Word order is typical Persian: prepositional phrase → object (نیایش) → verb (گرفت).
segments.s656-g18: جهان‌آفرین را places the indirect object in front of the noun phrase with را marking it as definite/direct object ('the Creator' as recipient of praise). ستایش گرفت repeats the same light-verb pattern: ستایش (noun 'praise') + گرفت (past 'took' used idiomatically 'offered/praised'). The overall clause means 'he offered praise to the Creator.'
segments.s656-g19: گهرکرد is used here as a proper name (the donor/agent) and functions as the subject of the clause. بسیار پیشش نثار is a short predicate: بسیار modifies نثار ('many' offerings), پیشش ('before him' / 'in his presence') gives the spatial/relational complement, and نثار (noun) is used as a verb-like nominal phrase meaning 'offered' (elliptical 'he offered many offerings'). Word order: subject → quantifier + adverbial → noun.
segments.s656-g20: This is a list of the specific gifts. یکی (numeral) precedes the noun چتر ('a single canopy'); و 'and' links the next item; ده پیل is 'ten elephants' (numeral before noun, standard Persian order). با introduces an instrument/feature 'with' and attaches to گوشوار ('ear-ornaments, earrings'), so 'ten elephants with earrings.' The whole phrase is a noun phrase enumerating gifts.
segments.s656-g21: This noun phrase modifies the canopy: بیاراسته here functions as an adjectival modifier meaning 'adorned' (classical usage equivalent to 'well-adorned' rather than a literal negative 'un-'), preceding the head noun چتر ('canopy'). هندی ('Indian') is another pre-nominal adjective specifying style/origin. به زر is a prepositional phrase indicating material/finish ('in/with gold'); in classical Persian such به + material often renders an adjectival sense ('golden' / 'gilt').
segments.s656-g22: بدو is a short prepositional pronominal phrase 'to it/for it' (referring back to the canopy). بافته is a participial adjective 'woven' (from بافتن) describing how it was made. چند گونه گهر is a quantifying noun phrase: چند ('several/some') + گونه ('kinds/types') + گهر ('gems/jewels'), i.e. 'with several kinds of jewels.' Word order: beneficiary → participle → quantifier + noun.
segments.s656-g23: سر بار is a nominal phrase literally 'the head/top of the load' (سر used to indicate the top/front portion). بگشاد is a past form meaning 'opened' (here used intransitively in the sense 'they opened/unpacked'); در بارگاه is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the royal court/assembly hall.' The sequence follows Persian order: object phrase → verb → locative phrase.
segments.s656-g24: بیاورد is a past-tense (literary) form of آوردن 'to bring' ('they brought'). یک سر همه is an idiomatic expression meaning 'each one, one by one' or 'one of everything' (یک ... همه here signals distribution/iteration across the items). نزد شاه is a prepositional phrase 'before the king / in the presence of the king.' The clause thus reports that the items were brought, individually, to the king.
segments.s656-g337: ازان آگهی is an adverbial phrase ‘from that news / upon that information’ placed before the verb. شاد شد is a past-tense compound meaning ‘became happy’ (شاد = adjective, شد = past of شدن ‘to become’). شهریار, the subject, follows the verb here because Ferdowsi uses poetic inversion (verb + subject) instead of the more neutral Persian Subject–Verb order.
segments.s656-g338: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past form meaning ‘he said/commanded’ and introduces speech or an order. The following token تاهرک is a proper name (object/recipient of the utterance). بد نامدار is a two-word descriptive phrase: بد (negative/ill) + نامدار (renowned); here it functions as an epithet ‘ill‑famed / notorious’ attached to the name.
segments.s656-g339: ز شهر و ز لشکر is a coordinated prepositional phrase ‘from the city and from the army’ (ز = poetic from/of). خبیره شدند is a past plural verbal phrase: خبیره (pl. of خبره/خبیر, ‘skilled/knowledgeable/experienced’) + شدند (past plural of شدن ‘to become / to be’), here meaning ‘(they) were/appeared as experienced ones’ or ‘knowledgeable ones came forward.’
segments.s656-g340: همه نامداران is the noun phrase ‘all the renowned ones’ (همه = all/everyone; نامداران = famous/illustrious persons). پذیره شدند is a passive/reciprocal-like construction ‘were received/welcomed’ (پذیره = reception/acceptance; شدند = became/were), so the whole clause means the famed were welcomed/received.
segments.s656-g341: به شهر اندر آمد is a spatial phrase + verb: به ... اندر آمد = ‘came into the city / entered the city’ (به + شهر + اندر + آمد). چنان ارجمند is a postposed descriptive phrase ‘so/such venerable’ modifying the arrival or the person; چنان is an intensifier/adverb and ارجمند is an adjective meaning ‘esteemed / august.’ The verb آمد is past tense.
segments.s656-g342: به پیروزی here introduces the circumstance or occasion, roughly ‘in/with the victory’ (poetic use of به). شهریار بلند is a genitival noun phrase ‘the lofty/high king’ (شهریار = king; بلند = lofty/high). Together the segment gives the circumstance of the arrival: during/after the king’s great victory.
segments.s656-g343: به ایوان چو آمد is a temporal/spatial clause: به ایوان = ‘to the iwan/portico,’ چو آمد = ‘when he came’ (چو = poetic ‘when’). به نزدیک تخت is an additional locative phrase ‘to/near the throne’ (به + نزدیک + تخت), specifying proximity to the throne at the time of arrival.
segments.s656-g344: برو here appears in the manuscript text where classical Persian would have بر او (‘upon him’); in Ferdowsi’s orthography such sequences can be compressed. Taken as بر او شهریار آفرین کرد سخت the structure is: (بر او) = ‘upon him,’ شهریار = subject ‘the king,’ آفرین کرد = ‘pronounced praise / praised’ (آفرین as noun/exclamation, کرد = did), and سخت is an adverbial intensifier ‘strongly / heartily.’ Thus the clause means ‘the king praised him greatly.’
segments.s656-g9: که introduces a relative/clausal link here meaning 'that'/'who'; آمد is the past tense verb 'came' placed before its nominal subject (classical/poetic word order); فرستاده is the head noun 'messenger' and شاه هند is a genitive construction 'king of India' (شاه modifies هند). Together this yields 'that the messenger of the King of India came.'
segments.s656-g10: This is a coordinated list of things accompanying the envoy: the initial token (ابا) appears here as an instrumental/associative element (likely a poetic/archaic form of 'با' = 'with' or a prosthetic alef), then a series of nouns joined by و 'and'; چتر is a literal canopy/umbrella, and سند postmodifies سواران to mean 'riders of Sind.' The whole phrase attaches to the previous segment as the things the messenger brought/with which he came.
segments.s656-g11: This NP describes the travelling entourage: شتروار is an adverbial/qualifying word ('in a camel‑train' / 'in a long file'), followed by بارست (here used as a noun/poetic term for the caravan/party) and the prepositional phrase با او 'with him'; the numeral هزار ('a thousand') quantifies the group. Word order is poetic: qualifier + group noun + 'with him' + numeral.
segments.s656-g12: همی is the classical progressive/adverbial marker meaning 'continually' or 'kept on'; راه is the direct object 'the road/way'; جوید is the present/stative verb 'seeks/makes for' (classical present form); بر شهریار is a prepositional phrase 'toward the king' (بر used as 'toward/to' in older usage). Together: 'kept making their way toward the king.'
segments.s656-g13: همانگه چو is a fixed temporal phrase 'as soon as' / 'when at that moment'; بشنید is the simple past 'he heard'; بیدار qualifies شاه and precedes it as an epithet ('the alert/awake king'). The clause reads 'as soon as the alert king heard.'
segments.s656-g14: پذیره here functions as a noun meaning 'reception/receiving' (expressing the purpose), followed by the verb فرستاد 'sent'; چندی سپاه is the object 'some/several troops.' The construction is purpose-noun + verb: 'he sent some troops for reception' (i.e. to receive/escort them).
segments.s656-g15: چو آمد is a temporal clause 'when ... came'; بر here functions as 'before/toward' in classical usage introducing the person approached; شهریار بزرگ is a nominal phrase 'the great king' with the adjective بزرگ following the noun. The clause means 'when he came before the great king.'
segments.s656-g16: فرستاده is the head noun 'messenger/envoy' and is followed by two epithets joined by و: نامدار 'renowned' and سترگ (an archaic/poetic epithet). The adjectives post-modify the noun in the poetic style: 'the renowned and ___ envoy.'
segments.s656-g249: This segment lists what he brought. The first token ابا is ambiguous/archaic here (see note) and then بار 'load' is the noun being described. با is the preposition 'with' linking the load to the following items (نامه 'letter' and تخت نرد 'backgammon board', a compound where تخت 'board/throne' combines with نرد 'nard/backgammon'). Word order is noun (بار) + prepositional phrase 'با نامه و تخت نرد' describing the contents of the load; the conjunction و is contracted in the orthography as وتخت.
segments.s656-g250: دلش پر ز + NP is a fixed pattern: دلِ-ش (دل + possessive clitic -ش 'his') + پر 'full (of)' + ز 'from/of' + بازار ننگ 'the market's shame'. The final ونبرد is the conjunction و + نبرد (past-tense negative of بردن) so it means 'and did not carry' or 'and would not bear'. The line thus links his outward baggage to an internal state (his heart full of shame) and states he did not carry/dispense that shame.
segments.s656-g251: چو آمد is a temporal clause 'when he came'; به نزدیکی (به + نزدیکی) means 'to/near the nearness' i.e. 'near/close to'. تخت اوی places the noun 'throne' before the possessive clitic اوی 'his' (in older/poetic Persian the clitic can follow the noun), so 'to his throne'. Overall: 'When he came near his throne...'.
segments.s656-g252: بدید is simple past 'he saw'. The list آن سر و افسر و بخت اوی strings three direct objects with و 'and': آن 'that/the' qualifies سر 'head', افسر 'helmet' (a metonym for crown), and بخت اوی 'his fortune' where اوی again is the possessive clitic 'his'. The adjectives/determiners precede the nouns in the expected order.
segments.s656-g253: فراوانش is فراوان + clitic -ش 'him' and here functions adverbially/quantifying the following verb: 'praised him greatly'. بستود is past 'praised'. بر پهلوی literally 'on/at the side' (i.e. 'beside him' or 'in his presence'); this is a locative phrase modifying the praising.
segments.s656-g254: بدو داد is the sequence 'gave to him' (بدو = 'to him', داد = 'gave'). پس here is temporal/sequence 'then' (introducing what follows). نامه خسروی is a noun phrase 'the royal letter' (نامه 'letter' modified by خسروی 'royal/kingly'). The whole clause: 'and then he gave him the king's letter.'
segments.s656-g255: This is a series introduced by the preposition ز 'concerning/from' applied to three topics joined by an older form 'وز' which here functions like و ز 'and ...' (an archaism: وَ + ز). The nouns شطرنج, راه, رنج are the topics; رای at the end of the series is the head noun 'counsel/opinion' that governs the clause: 'concerning chess, and the road, and the hardships, (he spoke) his counsel.' The sequence is topic-list + governing noun.
segments.s656-g256: بگفت is past 'he said/spoke'. آنچه آمد 'that which came' = 'what had happened'; یکایک is an adverb 'one by one' or 'each in turn'; به جای is an idiom here meaning 'in place'/'in order' or 'in its proper turn'. The clause therefore specifies how he reported events: 'he told, one by one and in order, whatever had happened.'
segments.s656-g329: نه کس دانشی‌تر ز دستور اوی is a comparative clause with an omitted copula. نه کس = “no one”; دانشی‌تر is the comparative formed with -تر on دانشی (‘learned/knowledgeable’), meaning “more learned.” ز + دستور اوی functions as the standard of comparison (‘than in/with regard to doctrine/rules’), and اوی is the third‑person pronoun (“he”) — the verb ‘is’ is omitted (typical in poetry).
segments.s656-g330: ز دانش سپهرست گنجور اوی: ز introduces a genitive/source relation (‘of/from’). دانش here means learnedness/learning, modified by سپهرست (‘heavenly, lofty’) as an adjectival compound; گنجور is predicative/adjectival (‘treasure‑laden’ or ‘a storehouse’), and اوی is the subject pronoun ‘he.’ The copula is again omitted, so literally ‘he is a treasure‑laden one of heavenly learning.’
segments.s656-g331: فرستاده شد باژ یک ساله پیش: فرستاده شد is a passive/perfect construction made of the past participle فرستاده + auxiliary شد meaning ‘was sent.’ باژ (‘a cry/summons’) and یک ساله پیش (‘one year before/ago’) are adverbial/temporal/instrumental phrases; word order is poetic (verb first, then these modifiers).
segments.s656-g332: اگر بیش باید فرستیم بیش is a conditional: اگر = ‘if’; بیش = ‘more’ (object/quantity); باید is a modal ‘must/it is necessary’; فرستیم is 1st‑person plural present/subjunctive ‘we send’ used after باید to express obligation. The final بیش repeats/emphasizes ‘more.’ The clause means ‘if more is needed, we must send more.’
segments.s656-g333: ز باژی که پیمان نهادیم نیز: ز introduces the instrumental/source (‘by/from that summons’). که introduces a relative clause modifying باژی: پیمان نهادیم is the simple past ‘we established/laid the covenant’ (نهادیم is past of نهادن). نیز = ‘also/likewise’ attaches after the clause for emphasis. Word order places the relative clause between head and the resumptive adverbial.
segments.s656-g334: فرستاده شد هرچ بایست چیز: again فرستاده شد = ‘was sent’ (passive). هرچ (‘whatever/whatever thing’) + بایست (‘ought/was necessary’) + چیز (‘thing’) form an indefinite nominal phrase ‘whatever thing ought to be (sent).’ The phrase order is inverted (poetic): literal ‘was sent whatever thing ought.’
segments.s656-g335: چو آگاهی آمد ز دانا به شاه: چو = temporal conjunction ‘when’; آگاهی آمد = ‘news/information came’ (simple past آمد). ز دانا indicates the source ‘from the sage/wise man’; به شاه indicates the recipient ‘to the king.’ Word order places source before recipient, typical in poetry.
segments.s656-g336: که با کام و با خوبی آمد ز راه: که introduces the content clause ‘that…’ با کام و با خوبی are parallel adverbial phrases introduced by با (‘with’), here meaning ‘in satisfaction/good spirit (کام)’ and ‘with goodness/benefit (خوبی).’ آمد = past ‘came’; ز راه = ‘by/along the road’ giving manner/direction. The clause reports how/with what favor he came.
segments.s656-g321: بیامد is a past‑tense, 3rd‑person singular verb (‘he came’); in classical/poetic Persian the verb may appear before the following prepositional phrase. ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’, so ز قنوج names the origin (‘from Kannauj’). بوزرجمهر is the proper name (the subject understood with بیامد).
segments.s656-g322: برافراخته is an adjectival/participial phrase ‘with (his) head raised’ (literally ‘raised’); سر is the noun ‘head’ and here forms the object/locative of the action. بگردان is used as a finite verb meaning ‘turned’ (an archaic/poetic form of ‘turn’), and سپهر is the directional/goal ‘the sky’ (the preposition به is omitted for poetic meter). The whole chunk means ‘with head raised, (he) turned to the sky.’
segments.s656-g323: دلی شاد is an adjectival phrase ‘glad of heart’ (دلی qualifying emotional state). با introduces the means/instrument ‘with’, governing نامه (‘a letter’). شاه هند is a genitival phrase ‘the king of India’ modifying نامه (i.e. ‘a letter of the king of India’). The word order places the emotional stance before the instrumental phrase.
segments.s656-g324: نبشته functions as a noun/participle ‘a written text’ (a written message) referring back to the نامه. به + هندی here marks language/style: به هندی = ‘in Hindī’ (i.e. in the Indian language or style). خطی is used in the sense ‘script/related to writing’ (so ‘in the Hindī script’). بر is the preposition ‘on/upon’, and پرند (‘bird’) is the surface/carrier — ‘written in Hindī script upon a bird’ (the image of a message attached to or written on a bird).
segments.s656-g325: که here introduces a subordinate/purpose clause (‘so that’). رای is ‘opinion/counsel’ (here meaning spoken counsel), و is the coordinating ‘and’, بزرگان ‘the great ones/elders’ (the speakers). گوایی is used as the notion of expressing or giving voice (poetically ‘eloquence’/‘uttering’), and دهند is the present/subjunctive/optative sense ‘may give’ or ‘they give’ (in context expressing purpose or result). Together: ‘so that the counsels and the elders may speak/declare.’
segments.s656-g326: نه از بیم is a negative phrase ‘not out of fear’ (نه negates the following idea; از denotes source/reason). کزنیک is a compact/poetic form meaning roughly ‘for good’/‘so that good (be done)’ or ‘so that good counsel be given’ (here indicating the positive motive/purpose). رایی is another form of ‘rāy’ (opinion/counsel), and دهند repeats ‘they give’ — the clause denies fear as the motive and asserts that the motive is to give good counsel.
segments.s656-g327: که چون here introduces an explanatory subordinate clause; چون = ‘as/like’ (comparing to something not seen before), and که acts as the subordinating particle ‘that/for’. شاه نوشین‌روان is the noun phrase ‘a king sweet‑souled’ (نوشین‌روان = pleasant‑minded/sweet‑souled). کس ندید is a negative past clause ‘no one saw’ (کس = anyone/someone used with the negative verb ندید meaning ‘nobody had seen’).
segments.s656-g328: نه is the coordinating negative ‘nor’ (continuing the negation). از marks the agent/source ‘from’, موبد سالخورده is a nominal phrase ‘an aged mōbed (Zoroastrian priest)’ (موبد = priest; سالخورده = elderly). شنید is the past tense ‘heard’ (here ‘nor had an old mōbed heard [of such a king]’). The clause parallels the previous ‘nobody had seen…’ by adding that even the old priest had not heard of such a king.
segments.s656-g113: بفرمود تا is an honorific-past verb plus the subordinator تا: بفرمود is an elevated/polite past meaning 'he commanded/said', and تا introduces the subordinate clause expressing what was ordered. موبدان و ردان are two coordinated groups (mōbeds and rādān) serving as the subject of the subordinate clause.
segments.s656-g114: برفتند is simple past, 3rd person plural 'they went/departed'. با marks accompaniment ('with'), and نامور بخردان is a noun phrase 'renowned wise‑ones' (نامور modifies بخردان); word order places the adjective before the noun but the whole phrase is the complement of با.
segments.s656-g115: فرستاده رای را is the definite object (را marks it): فرستاده رای 'the envoy/messenger of counsel' (رای modifying فرستاده). پیش خواند is a compound verb meaning 'called forward' (پیش here 'in the presence/of before' + خواند 'called/summoned').
segments.s656-g116: بران is a demonstrative/pronominal adverb referring back to that person ('that one'/'thereon'), and نامور qualifies him as 'renowned'. پیشگاهش is 'his court/presence' (پیشگاه + possessive suffix), and نشاند is an archaic past meaning 'he caused (him) to sit' — i.e. 'seated him' (transitive).
segments.s656-g117: This is inverted, literary word order: بدو 'to him' (indirect object) + گفت (past 'said') + گوینده بوزرجمهر 'the speaker Bozorgmehr' (appositive/agent). In modern order it reads 'گوینده بوزرجمهر بدو گفت' — 'the orator Bozorgmehr said to him.'
segments.s656-g118: که‌ای here introduces direct address (vocative 'O' + relative particle), followed by موبد رای خورشید چهر, a noun phrase: موبد 'mōbed/priest', رای 'counsel' (qualifying the mōbed), and خورشید چهر a compound adjective 'sun‑faced' (خورشید = sun, چهر = face) meaning 'of sun‑like visage/bright-faced'. The whole functions as a vocative: 'O priest, whose counsel is sun‑faced...'.
segments.s656-g119: ازین مهرها is 'from these loves/affections' (از + این [here spelled ین] + مهرها). رای is 'counsel' (the thing given), با توچه is a prepositional phrase literally 'with regard to you/care for you' (با + توچه 'attention/care'), and گفت is the past verb 'said' (here marking that he gave such counsel).
segments.s656-g120: که introduces the content of the counsel ('that/namely'). همواره 'always/ever'. با توخرد is 'with thy wisdom' or 'in companionship with prudence' (توخرد is an older/compound form meaning 'thy wisdom' or 'with wisdom toward you'). باد is the optative particle 'may it be', and جفت 'a pair/companion' — together yielding the wish 'may prudence always be paired with you.'
segments.s656-g185: به فرمان ایشان is an agent/instrument phrase meaning “by their command” (به + فرمان + ایشان). The noun سپاه (army) follows and is the grammatical subject; از دو روی (“from/both sides”) is a prepositional phrase placed after the subject for emphasis. Word order is poetic (instrument → subject → source).
segments.s656-g186: به تندی is an adverbial phrase meaning “with speed/quickly” (به + noun used adverbially). بیاراسته is a poetic past-participial adjective modifying جنگجوی, here meaning ‘arrayed/armed’ (an archaic/poetic form functioning as a participle). جنگجوی is the noun ‘warriors’ which the participle describes.
segments.s656-g187: یکی را is a definite/object-marked noun phrase (‘one/him’ with را marking the direct object). چوتنها = چو + تنها ‘when (he is) alone’ (a temporal/conditional clause). بگیرد is a present/optative form used narratively here ‘seize’ and the subject دو تن follows the verb (poetic verb–subject order): “two men seize him when he is alone.”
segments.s656-g188: ز لشکر is a poetic prepositional phrase ‘from the host/army’ (ز = from). برین is the fused locative/pointing element (‘toward these/thereon’ — here acting adverbially), followed by یک تن (one man) as subject and آید شکن ‘comes as a breaker’ where شکن is a substantive role ‘breaker/champion’ and آید is the verb 'comes forward'.
segments.s656-g189: به هرجای = 'to/at every place' (here 'throughout/everywhere'); پیش و پس is a fixed pair meaning 'front and back' or 'before and behind' (وپس written together in poetry); اندر سپاه = 'within the host/army' (اندر = in/within). The phrase as a whole adverbially locates the action throughout the army, front and rear.
segments.s656-g190: گرازان is the plural noun 'boars' used metaphorically; دو شاه is the noun phrase 'two kings' (subject); اندرَان (here spelled اندرَان) + رزمگاه places them 'therein/among them in the battlefield' (اندران = therein/among them, رزمگاه = battlefield). The clause says the two kings fought like boars in that field.
segments.s656-g191: همی is an iterative/adverbial particle 'continuously/kept on'. این بران آن برین is a paired contrast: این … بران (this on one side) و آن … برین (that on the other); برگذشت is a past/perfective verb 'passed by' or 'went past'. The sequence describes a repeated mutual passing: 'this on one side, that on the other, kept passing.'
segments.s656-g192: گهی … گهی is a paired adverbial expression meaning 'sometimes … sometimes …'. رزم is the noun 'battle' and is repeated with two locatives: کوه = 'mountain' and دشت = 'plain', so the clause means 'sometimes the fighting (was) in the mountains, and sometimes in the plains.' The verb is elided and supplied from context.
segments.s656-g353: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause ('when'). آن نامه رای هندی is a noun phrase where نامه ('letter') is qualified by رای هندی ('a counsel/advice of Indian origin' or 'Indian counsel'); بخواند is a third-person singular past/narrative verb form meaning 'he read/recited' that completes the temporal clause.
segments.s656-g354: یکی here means 'one' / 'a certain' and introduces the affected group (an assembly). انجمن is the noun 'assembly.' درشگفتی is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'in astonishment,' and بماند is third-person singular past/narrative 'remained' (i.e. 'was left').
segments.s656-g355: هم is an additive adverb 'also.' از here marks the cause or object of astonishment ('at' / 'because of'). دانش و رای is a coordinated noun phrase 'learning and counsel.' بوزرجمهر names the wise minister whose learning/counsel are being referred to.
segments.s656-g356: ازان is a demonstrative prepositional marker 'from that' or 'at that,' introducing a second cause of amazement. بخت is 'fortune' or 'fate,' and سالار خورشید‌چهر is a compound title 'the lord/master (سالار) with a sunlike face' (i.e. the sun‑faced sovereign); the whole phrase means 'that fortune of the sun‑faced sovereign.'
segments.s656-g357: چنین گفت کسری is a formulaic reporting clause 'thus Khosrow said.' The following که یزدان سپاس functions as an exclamatory/subordinate clause meaning 'that God be praised' (i.e. 'praise be to God').
segments.s656-g358: The second که introduces the content clause 'that I am ...' هستم is first‑person singular present 'I am.' خردمند and نیکی‌شناس are predicate nouns meaning 'wise' and 'one who knows/recognizes goodness' respectively; و simply coordinates them.
segments.s656-g359: مهان is a plural noun here meaning 'nobles' / 'great ones' (often 'guests' but used honorifically). تاج و تخت 'crown and throne' are a coordinated noun phrase. مرا is the object/dative 'to me' (written here split as م + را in the lineation), and بنده‌اند is third‑person plural 'they are servants' or 'they serve.' The clause states that these powers/people serve the speaker.
segments.s656-g360: دل و جان 'heart and life' (i.e. 'hearts and souls') is the subject; به مهر من is a prepositional phrase 'with love for me' ('مهر' = love/affection, 'من' = me/my). آگنده‌اند is third‑person plural 'are filled,' so the clause means 'hearts and lives are filled with love for me.'
segments.s656-g105: به خوبی همه بازی آمد به جای is a compact clause meaning “everything turned out well / the whole affair came right.” به + خوبی forms an adverbial phrase “well” (به as an adverbial marker + خوبی ‘goodness’). همه بازی is a DP ‘the whole affair/entire game,’ آمد is the past/present narrative verb ‘came/happened,’ and به جای (‘into place’) is a prepositional phrase indicating result.
segments.s656-g106: This short NP 'به بخت بلند جهان کدخدای' specifies for whom things fell into place: به = 'for/to', بخت بلند = 'high/lofty fortune' (an epithet), جهان qualifies the following noun ('of the world' / 'of the realm'), and کدخدای is the headman/title. Note the lack of a copula: the phrase is an indirect object/beneficiary phrase linked to the previous clause.
segments.s656-g107: فرستاده شاه را پیش خواه literally places فرستاده شاه ('the king's messenger') with the object marker را before the verb خواه ('wants/desires'). پیش here means 'forward / into presence.' The verb خواه is the classical form of خواستن, here expressing desire/command: 'he wished/ordered that the king's messenger go forward.'
segments.s656-g108: کسی را که دارند ما را نگاه is a relative clause functioning as the object of خواه: '(for) someone who will keep/watch over us.' کسی را is the indefinite object marked by را; که introduces the relative clause; دارند is present 'they have / will have' used here to express possession/continuing action in the relative clause; ما را نگاه combines the object pronoun + نگاه ('watch/guard') to mean 'watch over us.'
segments.s656-g109: شهنشاه باید که بیند نخست: باید 'must/ought' takes the subjunctive clause introduced by که + بیند (subjunctive of دیدن) to mean 'must see.' The subject شهریار/شهنشاه follows the predicate here (common in poetic narration), and نخست 'first' indicates order.
segments.s656-g110: یکی رزمگاهست گویی درست: یکی 'a certain/one' + رزمگاهست is a fused noun+copula ('is a battlefield')—-ست = 'is.' گویی here functions parenthetically ('as if' / 'it seems'), modifying the following درست ('proper/exactly'); the clause thus means the place appears to be a proper battlefield. (The sense entry provided for گویی did not include its 'it seems/as if' usage, so that sense is marked separately.)
segments.s656-g111: ز گفتار او شاد شد شهریار is inverted: ز گفتار او 'from his speech' (z = poetic 'from/because of') is an agent/cause phrase; شاد شد 'became glad' is the verbal predicate; شهریار (the king) appears after the verb for emphasis but is the grammatical subject.
segments.s656-g112: ورا نیک‌پی خواند و به روزگار: ورا = 'to him' (object pronoun), نیک‌پی is an epithet/title meaning 'virtuous / well‑born' used as the name he gives, and خواند here means 'called/named' (خواند can also mean 'read' or 'sing', but context shows 'to name'). و به روزگار begins a new phrase 'and in time/at that time...' and is left open to continue the narrative.
segments.s656-g313: The clause is a simple past-tense clause: یکی (a certain) + افسری (a diadem/helmet as direct object) + خواست (he asked/requested). The prepositional phrase از گنج رای follows خواست and gives source/authority ('from the treasure/treasury/counsel of the treasury'); رای here means 'counsel/plan' or 'the treasury's decision' and links as the origin of the item.
segments.s656-g314: This is a noun phrase in apposition or specification: همان (that/the same) qualifies جامه (garment/robe), زر (gold) is used attributively ('golden'), then the poetic preposition ز (from) + سر تا به پای gives extent 'from head to foot' (سر = head; تا ... به پای = as far as/ down to the foot). Word order follows classical Persian poetic style (adjective after noun; poetic ز for از).
segments.s656-g315: بدو (be + du) is the dative clitic phrase 'to him'; داد is the past of دادن 'gave.' و چند آفرین کرد is a coordinate clause: و چند ('and a few/several') + آفرین ('acclamation/bravo') + کرد ('he made/uttered'). نیز at the end means 'also/likewise.' The sequence describes both the giving and the spoken praise.
segments.s656-g316: This clause means 'he bestowed many things on his companions.' بیارانش functions as the indirect object ('on his companions' or 'to his companions') and is followed by بخشید (past of بخشیدن in the sense 'to give/bestow'), then بسیار چیز 'many things' (بسیار = many, چیز = things). Note: بیارانش is used here as a recipients phrase; the exact morpheme is archaic/poetic.
segments.s656-g317: This short clause states the movement/number: شتر دو هزار (two thousand camels) is the object phrase, آنک is a relative/connector ('that/which'), از پیش ('from/before') gives temporal/spatial nuance, and برد is the past of بردن 'took/led away' (classical poetic past). The overall sense: he carried/led away two thousand camels forward.
segments.s656-g318: ابا باژ here functions as a proper name (Abā-Bāzh). The coordinate و links to هدیه (gift) and the clause مر او را سپرد is 'entrusted him to me' — مر = 'to me', او = 'him', را = object marker, سپرد = past of سپردن 'entrusted/handed over.' Thus: 'Abā-Bāzh entrusted him and his gift to me.'
segments.s656-g319: یکی کاروان بد is the indefinite noun phrase 'a certain/bad caravan' (یکی = a certain, کاروان = caravan, بد = bad). که introduces a relative clause: کس پیش ازان 'no one before that' (کس = anyone/person, پیش = before, ازان = of/than that). The clause sets up who had or had not acted toward the caravan.
segments.s656-g320: This is the continuation of the relative clause with negation: نراند is negative past (ن‑ prefix + راندن) 'did not drive away'; و = 'and'; نبد is an archaic negative past of بودن (≈ 'there was not'); خواسته is the noun/participle 'desire/wish' and بیش ازان = 'more than that.' Together: 'no one before drove (them) off, and there had not been a wish/claim greater than that.'
segments.s656-g97: بیاورد is a past 3rd‑person form (verb first, as in classical Persian) meaning 'brought'; the direct object شطرنج follows the verb. The subject بوزرجمهر comes after the object (V O S order), so the whole segment reads 'Bozorgmehr brought (out) chess'.
segments.s656-g98: پراندیشه is an adjectival/adverbial phrase 'full of thought, pensive' modifying the following verb. بنشست is the simple past 'he sat down'; و links the two actions. بگشاد is an older/perhaps poetic past form with a prefixed b- meaning 'opened/became open', and چهر is the direct object 'face/visage' — together 'and he opened/brightened his face' (i.e. smiled or relaxed his expression).
segments.s656-g99: همی‌جست contains the habitual/continuous prefix همی- + جست (kept seeking/playing), a classical imperfective sense 'kept at' or 'kept examining'. بازی is the object 'the game'; چپ و دست راست are locative/directional modifiers 'left and (the) right hand' describing how he played or examined the game. Word order is verb → object → modifiers.
segments.s656-g100: همی‌راند is another habitual/continuous past action 'he kept moving/was moving (pieces)'. تا introduces a result/purpose clause 'until' and governs the subordinate clause جای هریک کجاست, where جای هریک = 'each one's place' and کجاست is an indirect interrogative/copular clause 'where it is' (کجا + ست, copula at the end).
segments.s656-g101: به یک روز و یک شب gives a temporal span 'after/for one day and one night' (به used here to mark time/duration). چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بازیش is 'his play/game' with the clitic -ش, and یافت (past 3rd) 'was found/was fulfilled' functions idiomatically as 'when his game was over' or 'when his play had come to an end.'
segments.s656-g102: از ... شتافت is a prepositional phrase + past verb: از ایوان سوی شاه ایران = 'from the portico toward the king of Iran' (سوی = toward(s)), and شتافت is the past 3rd 'he hastened/ran.' The sequence places the source and direction before the verb as typical in classical narration.
segments.s656-g103: بدو is the dative/clitic meaning 'to him' (introducing the indirect object), and گفت is the past 'he said'. کای is the vocative particle 'O', used to address someone directly; شاه پیروزبخت = 'fortunate/prosperous king' is the addressee. The order is 'to him he said: O fortunate king...' (introducing direct speech).
segments.s656-g104: نگه کردم is a light-noun + کردن construction where نگه ('a look/gaze') + کردم ('I did') yields 'I looked' or 'I have looked/seen'. The following coordinated noun phrase این مهره و مشک و تخت lists the objects 'these piece(s), the musk, and the throne'. کردَم is 1st‑person past, so this is reported speech: 'I have looked upon these pieces and the musk and the throne.'
segments.s656-g121: چنین داد پاسخ = a poetic inversion of the more common 'پاسخ چنین داد' or 'چنین پاسخ داد' and means 'he answered thus'. داد is the past-tense verb 'gave/said' and که introduces the reported speech that follows; فرخنده‌رای is an epithet addressed to the addressee ('fortunate/auspicious-minded one') placed after the complementizer for emphasis.
segments.s656-g122: چو ... من برفتم ز جای = 'when I left/rose from the place/in his presence'. چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'; از پیش او is a prepositional phrase 'from before him/in his presence'; من is the nominative pronoun 'I'; برفتم is an archaic/poetic past verb meaning 'I departed/rose/moved away' (the provided lemma list doesn't match this verbal sense); ز is the poetic form of از 'from'; جای = 'place'.
segments.s656-g123: مرا گفت = 'he said to me' with the object pronoun (مرا) placed before the verb (a normal Persian order). کین مهره ساج و عاج = 'this bead/amulet of saj and ivory' — کین here is an archaic demonstrative 'this' modifying مهره ('bead, amulet'), followed by the materials ساج and عاج joined by و.
segments.s656-g124: ببر = imperative 'take!/bring!' (an order whose direct object is the bead just mentioned). پیش تخت خداوند = prepositional phrase 'before the throne of the lord/sovereign' (پیش = 'before/in the presence of', تخت خداوند = 'the sovereign's throne'). تاج at the end is appositive/emphatic ('the crown') closely associated with تخت or serving as a vocative/emphatic word in the command.
segments.s656-g125: بگویش = imperative 'tell him' formed from بگو + third-person enclitic -یش ('to him'). که introduces the content clause. با موبد و رای‌زن = 'with the mobed and the counselor' describing who should be involved; موبد is the Zoroastrian priest and رای‌زن is a counselor/advisor.
segments.s656-g126: بنه پیش و بنشان یکی انجمن = two imperatives in sequence: بنه (place/put; here an imperative whose lexical senses in the supplied list don't match the verbal use) + پیش ('before/in the presence of'), و = 'and', بنشان = imperative 'seat (them)' (again the supplied lemma senses are lexical roots, not the verb sense), one انجمن = 'a single assembly/meeting' (یکی انجمن = 'one assembly'). The pair of verbs directs placing and seating to form one gathering.
segments.s656-g127: گر = conditional 'if'. این نغز بازی = 'this clever/delicate play/trick' where نغز qualifies بازی. به جای آورند is an idiomatic verbal cluster: به جای آوردن 'to carry out/perform/bring into place' — آورند is the third-person plural present/subjunctive form used here in a classical register meaning 'they perform/bring about'.
segments.s656-g128: پسندیده و دلربای آورند = 'they will make/produce [it] pleasing and heart-capturing.' پسندیده and دلربای are predicative/adjectival complements describing the result; آورند repeats the verb 'they bring/make' in the same third-person plural form, expressing the result that follows the conditional.
segments.s656-g257: پیام شهنشاه (noun + title) functions as the subject: “the emperor's message/envoy.” با او بگفت is a verbal phrase meaning “spoke/said to him”; با here (classical/poetic usage) marks the person to whom something is said (“to/with him”). بگفت is past tense with the older poetic prefix ب‑ + گفت (simple past) meaning “said/told.”
segments.s656-g258: This clause describes a reaction: رخ (face/countenance) + رای هندی (= “the counsel/plan of the Indian”) forms a noun phrase where رای is the idea/counsel and هندی qualifies it. چوگل is poetic simile “like a flower.” برشگفت is a past/punctual verbal form (بر‑شگفت) used poetically to indicate a sudden change — here best rendered as “bloomed/brightened/was astonished” of the face. Word order is noun phrase then predicate, common in classical Persian.
segments.s656-g259: بگفت introduces reported speech (“he said/asked”). آن (= “that”) points back to the preceding matter. کجا دید is an interrogative clause with verb after question‑word: کجا = “where,” دید = past tense “saw / perceived.” پاینده مرد is a post‑nominal adjective + noun meaning “the ever‑lasting/immortal man.” The phrase is a rhetorical question: “Where did that immortal man see that?”
segments.s656-g260: چنان هم = “in such a way / likewise” (چنان + emphatic هم). سراسر بیاورد uses سراسر (“entire/throughout”) with بیاورد (present/subjunctive/optative form of آورد with poetic ب‑) to mean “bring about/produce entirely.” نرد here names the game backgammon; the clause asks rhetorically whether he could so produce the whole game. The verb form conveys potential/ability in rhetorical question rather than a pure simple present.
segments.s656-g261: ز ... و از ... coordinates prepositional phrases: ز/از both mean “from.” بازی = “the game,” مهره = “game piece/pawn” (here gaming pieces), رای شاه = “the king's counsel/decision” (noun + governing noun). The sequence lists sources/items under consideration (game, pieces, and king's plan).
segments.s656-g262: وزان is the fused form of و + ز آن (“and from that”), linking the prior list to what follows. موبدان is plural of موبد (mōbed), i.e. Zoroastrian priests; نماینده is an appositive meaning “representative/agent.” راه here is “the Way”/religious path or method. The whole segment names the priests as representatives of the Way, deriving authority from the things just listed.
segments.s656-g263: به نامه دورن is a prepositional phrase: به + نامه (“into/onto the book/record”) and دورن (dourn) = “of the ages/remote time” — literally “the book of ages” or “the distant record.” آنچه کردست is a relative clause: آنچه = “that which/whatever” and کردست is an older perfect/past form (“has done”/“did”), so the whole means “inscribe/record whatever he has done.” یاد here functions as a noun “remembrance/record” (i.e. what is to be set down or remembered).
segments.s656-g264: This sequence gives the resulting actions and purpose: بخواند (3rd‑person jussive/optative of خواندن) = “let him read/recite,” بداند = “let him know/so that he know” (purpose/result), نپیچد is negative jussive of پیچیدن meaning “not turn aside/swerve,” and ز داد = “from justice” (ز = “from,” داد = “justice”). The construction expresses a hortatory instruction: read it, know it, and do not deviate from justice.
segments.s656-g129: A nominal list introduced by the demonstrative имени همین ('this / these very') followed by four names or poetic nouns joined with و. In classical Persian such a list is simply juxtaposed; همین points to the exact items being sent. The items (بدره، برده، باژ، ساو) here function as proper/poetic names (likely names of objects or pieces) rather than ordinary common nouns.
segments.s656-g130: فرستیم is the 1st-person-plural past (narrative) ‘we sent’. چندانک functions as a comparative/quantifier meaning ‘to that extent / as many as’, and داردیم (داریم) ‘we have’ gives the measure: together they mean ‘we sent as many as we had’. تاو is the object/measure-word here (a poetic noun). Word order is verb (فرستیم) followed by the quantifying clause that specifies how many were sent.
segments.s656-g131: و‌گر is the conditional particle ‘if’. The subject phrase شاه و فرزانگان ‘the king and the sages’ follows, and این به جای is the object phrase ‘this in (its) place’. The clause sets up a condition whose verbs appear in the next segment; word order is standard: conditional marker + subject + object/locative phrase.
segments.s656-g132: This segment contains two predicates. نیارند functions as a negative/negative-future or present-polite verb meaning roughly ‘they do not put / they do not place’ (here used of not setting something in its place). روشن qualifies رای: روشن رای = ‘clear opinion/counsel’. ندارند is the existential/possessive verb ‘they do not have’. The sequence therefore means ‘they do not place it, (and) they do not have a clear counsel.’
segments.s656-g133: نباید is an impersonal modal ‘it should not / must not’. که introduces the subordinate clause. خواهد is a 3rd-person verb here meaning ‘he desires / wishes’; ز ما marks the source ‘from us’; باژ و گنج are the objects 'Bāj and treasure'. The structure is negation + complementizer + verb phrase with source and objects: ‘It must not be that he desires from us Bāj and treasure.’
segments.s656-g134: دریغ is a noun ‘pity / regret’ that begins a clause of consequence. آیدش is آید + enclitic -ش ‘it comes to him’ (i.e. ‘befalls him’). جان دانا is a genitival phrase ‘the life (جان) of the wise (دانا)’ (dana qualifies jan), and به رنج means ‘into suffering / to hardship’. Together: ‘it would be a pity if the wise man’s life came to suffering.’
segments.s656-g135: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’. بیند is 3rd-person-subjunctive/present of دیدن ‘he sees’. دل و رای باریک ما is a noun phrase ‘our delicate/subtle heart and counsel’ where باریک (‘delicate, subtle, fine’) qualifies the preceding nouns and ما is the possessive ‘our’. The clause means ‘when he sees our fine heart and counsel’.
segments.s656-g136: فزونتر is the comparative/adverbial ‘more / in greater measure’. فرستد is the 3rd-person singular present/future ‘he sends’. به نزدیک ما is the prepositional phrase ‘to near us / to our proximity’. Order here is adverb – verb – prepositional complement: ‘he sends more to our vicinity.’
segments.s656-g1: چنین گفت موبد is a short reported-speech frame: چنین (thus) + گفت (past of گفتن, 'said') + موبد (the speaker). The conjunction که introduces the reported clause that follows; یک روز is an adverbial ('one day') that belongs to that subordinate clause and شاه is its subject.
segments.s656-g2: به + noun marks the manner/garment ('in/with brocade'); دیبای رومی is a genitival phrase 'Roman brocade/silk'. بیاراست is a literary past (from آراستن) 'he arrayed/put on'; گاه here is a temporal adverb reinforcing 'یک روز' (on that occasion/at that time).
segments.s656-g3: بیاویخت is a past-tense verb (literary form of آویختن) 'he hung/placed'. تاج is its direct object. از بر تخت عاج is a classical prepositional phrase meaning 'on/at the (ivory) throne' or 'from upon the ivory throne' (poetic uses of از بر); عاج ('ivory') qualifies تخت (throne).
segments.s656-g4: همه جای + noun is an idiom 'every part / everywhere of ...'. The line uses parallelism (همه جای عاج و همه جای تاج) to say that ivory covered every part of the throne and likewise (ivory or ornament) covered every part of the crown; جای functions as 'part/place' and the repetition emphasizes total coverage.
segments.s656-g5: همه کاخ پر + X is 'the whole palace was full of X' — پر here is used predicatively 'full (of)'. موبد و مرزبان are the occupants (priests and frontier-governors). Word order is noun (کاخ) + predicate (پر) + complement (موبد و مرزبان), a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s656-g6: ز lists origins: 'from Balkh and from Bamin and from Karzban.' The preposition ز is repeated for rhythmic/poetic emphasis. The final item کرزبان here is best read as a proper name/place in this enumeration.
segments.s656-g7: چنین آگهی یافت is 'thus received news' (آگهی 'information/news', یافت past of یافتن 'to receive/find'). شاه جهان is an epithet 'king of the world' serving as the subject that 'received' the information; word order places the adverbial/introductory چنین before the verb.
segments.s656-g8: ز introduces the source: 'from the speech/words (گفتار) of بیدار کارآگهان.' بیدار ('awake/alert') qualifies کارآگهان ('the informed/knowledgeable ones'); together they mean 'the alert, well‑informed men' whose speech provided the information.
segments.s656-g305: که is the relative/conjunction 'that/for' introducing a clause. اینت is a dialectal/poetic demonstrative or particle (here marking 'this one') and is irregular/archaic. سخنگوی is a deverbal noun 'speaker/utterance' used here as 'the saying' or 'speaker'; داننده is a participial noun 'knowing/learned (one)'; مرد is the noun 'man/person' completing the noun phrase — word order is demonstrative/descriptor + noun phrase, typical of epic Persian inversion.
segments.s656-g306: نه ... indicates negation 'not'. از بهر is a fixed prepositional phrase 'for/for the sake of' (از + بهر), followed by the objects of that purpose. شطرنج و بازی نرد lists two games; نرد (backgammon) is specified by بازی 'game'.
segments.s656-g307: بیاورد is a past tense verb 'brought/caused to come' (3rd-person in epic narrative). زان پس is a temporal adverbial 'from that, after that' (زان 'from that' + پس 'after/then'), and شتر دو هزار shows poetic inversion of the numeral and noun: the numeral follows the noun, giving 'two thousand camels.'
segments.s656-g308: همه is 'all/every'; گنج 'treasure' names what is loaded. قنوح is a proper name (here the owner/source of the treasure). کردند is the past plural 'they made/did' used causatively with بار 'load' to mean 'they loaded (it)'; بار is the noun 'load/cargo.'
segments.s656-g309: Repeated preposition ز (poetic for از) distributes 'from/with' over a list of goods: عود (oud), عنبر (ambergris), کافور (camphor) and زر (gold). The repetition is rhetorical and typical of classical Persian lists.
segments.s656-g310: همه 'all' quantifies the following nouns. جامه 'garment' and (وجام) here functions as 'and goblet' (the orthography joins و with جام); پیکر 'figure/body' and گهر 'jewel' complete the inventory. The phrase is a noun list describing what was packed — word order is noun phrases in series with an initial quantifier.
segments.s656-g311: ابا باژ appears as a compound name or label (here treated as a proper name or epithet) and is not being used in its lexical senses; therefore the supplied lexical senses do not apply. یکساله is an adjectival age 'one‑year‑old' modifying that name. از پیشگاه is the prepositional phrase 'from the presence/royal court' (از 'from' + پیشگاه 'presence/court').
segments.s656-g312: فرستاد is the simple past 'sent' (3rd person). یک سر is an indefinite accusative 'one head' (سر here likely literal 'head' or a trophy/gift); به is the preposition 'to/for'; درگاه شاه is the goal 'the king's court/threshold' with درگاه used in the sense of royal court. The clause is a simple past transitive construction: Subject (ابا باژ یکساله) + فرستاد + object + destination.
segments.s656-g201: دل شاه ایران is a possessive noun phrase in the order 'possessed + possessor' (literally 'heart, the Shah of Iran'), so: 'the Shah of Iran's heart/mind.' ازو is a source/causal particle ('from/by him') and خیره ماند is an adjectival phrase (خیره 'astonished/dazed' + ماند 'remained') meaning 'remained astonished' — a poetic past-state construction.
segments.s656-g202: خرد را is a direct object marked by را ('the reason/intellect'); باندیشه اندر is a prepositional phrase 'into/with thought' (با + اندیشه + اندر) placed before the verb for poetic meter; نشاند is an archaic/transitive past form meaning 'placed/settled' (third person) — together 'he set/placed the intellect into thought/contemplation.'
segments.s656-g203: همی‌گفت is a continuous/imperfect past verbal form 'kept saying' (common in epic style). کای is a vocative particle 'O,' here addressing the addressee; مرد روشن‌روان is a noun + adjective compound 'man of bright soul/cheerful spirit' with adjective after the noun as usual in Persian.
segments.s656-g204: This is an elliptic benedictory/wish: the copula is omitted for metrical/poetic effect. جوان بادی literally 'young/young‑hearted like the wind' (an idiomatic simile) and روزگارت جوان is 'may your days/fortune be youthful' — روزگارت is 'your time/fortune' with the possessive -ت implicit; و simply links the two parallel wishes.
segments.s656-g205: بفرمود is an honorific/past verb 'he commanded/said' (used for a king's utterance/command). تا here introduces the subordinate clause of command/purpose ('that/so that'); ساروان دو هزار places the noun 'sāravān' ('caravan‑leaders' or camel drivers) before the numeral for poetic word order ('two thousand caravan‑leaders').
segments.s656-g206: بیارد is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'bring' (third person subjunctive/optative used after تا for a commanded or wished action). شتر is the object 'camels.' The second تا + در شهریار functions adverbially 'to (the) court / into the shah's presence' with در 'in/at' marking the royal location.
segments.s656-g207: ز introduces origin/source repetitively ('from'). باری here means 'a host/force' or 'a burden/array' (poetic use) and که خیزد is a relative clause 'that rises/comes forth' with خیزد (present/habitual/epic present) 'arises' or 'begins.' The repeated ز ... و ز ... lists origins (Rūm and Chīn).
segments.s656-g208: A continuation of the same enumerative pattern: ز before each place ('from Hital and Makran and Iran‑land') — هیتال and مکران are place‑names, and ایران‌زمین is a compound meaning 'the land/realm of Iran.' The syntax is a simple coordinate list introduced by the prepositional z.
segments.s656-g289: بزرگان دانا is a nominal phrase in which بزرگان (the plural noun) is qualified by the adjective دانا; word order is Noun + Adjective (poetic/normal). به یک‌سو شدند is a past, intransitive clause: the preposition به + adverbial phrase یک‌سو (‘to one side’) and شدند (3rd person plural past of شدن) expressing motion/change of state = 'moved/withdrew.'
segments.s656-g290: به نادانی خویش is a prepositional/causal phrase meaning 'by/with their own ignorance'; خویش is the possessive expressed as خوی + ش (the possessive clitic). خستو شدند combines the adjective خستو ('weary') with شدند (3rd pl. past of شدن) to mean 'they became/grew weary' — an adjectival result/state formed with شدن.
